name: NIST Cybersecurity Framework
description: A voluntary framework of cybersecurity best practices and risk management guidelines, widely adopted across industries and now applicable to all organizations — not just critical infrastructure.

highlights: Added new ID.SC category — Supply Chain Risk Management — with 5 dedicated subcategories (ID.SC-1 through ID.SC-5)
Expanded authentication coverage in PR.AC with two new subcategories: identity proofing (PR.AC-6) and risk-based authentication (PR.AC-7)
Opened scope language to all organizations, not just critical infrastructure, setting the stage for the broader 2.0 rewrite
Added self-assessment guidance to help organizations measure their own cybersecurity maturity
Updated informative references throughout to reflect newer standards including SP 800-53 Rev 4 and COBIT 5
Clarified identity management, authentication, and access control definitions across multiple subcategories

changes.title: New ID.SC category: Supply Chain Risk Management
changes.newText: The organization's priorities, constraints, risk tolerances, and assumptions are established and used to support risk decisions associated with managing supply chain risk. The organization has established and implemented the processes to identify, assess and manage supply chain risks.
changes.rationale: Supply chain attacks were an emerging and underaddressed risk at the time of v1.0. NIST added this entire category to give organizations a structured way to manage cybersecurity risks originating from suppliers, vendors, and third-party service providers.

changes.title: ID.SC-1: Cyber supply chain risk management processes established
changes.area: ID — Identify / ID.SC — Supply Chain Risk Management
changes.newText: Cyber supply chain risk management processes are identified, established, assessed, managed, and agreed to by organizational stakeholders.
changes.rationale: Organizations needed explicit direction to establish formal processes — not just ad hoc practices — for managing risk across their supply chains.

changes.title: ID.SC-2: Suppliers and third parties identified and prioritized
changes.area: ID — Identify / ID.SC — Supply Chain Risk Management
changes.newText: Suppliers and third party partners of information systems, components, and services are identified, prioritized, and assessed using a cyber supply chain risk assessment process.
changes.rationale: Organizations cannot manage risk they haven't mapped. This subcategory requires explicit identification and prioritization of all supply chain participants.

changes.title: ID.SC-3: Contracts include cybersecurity requirements for suppliers
changes.area: ID — Identify / ID.SC — Supply Chain Risk Management
changes.newText: Contracts with suppliers and third-party partners are used to implement appropriate measures designed to meet the objectives of an organization's cybersecurity program and Cyber Supply Chain Risk Management Plan.
changes.rationale: Contractual requirements are the primary mechanism for enforcing cybersecurity expectations on third parties. This subcategory formalizes that obligation.

changes.title: ID.SC-4: Suppliers routinely assessed
changes.area: ID — Identify / ID.SC — Supply Chain Risk Management
changes.newText: Suppliers and third-party partners are routinely assessed using audits, test results, or other forms of evaluations to confirm they are meeting their contractual obligations.
changes.rationale: Contracts alone are insufficient — organizations need to actively verify that suppliers are meeting their security obligations through ongoing assessment.

changes.title: ID.SC-5: Response and recovery planning includes supply chain partners
changes.area: ID — Identify / ID.SC — Supply Chain Risk Management
changes.newText: Response and recovery planning and testing are conducted with suppliers and third-party providers.
changes.rationale: A supply chain incident requires coordinated response across multiple organizations. This subcategory ensures that supply chain partners are explicitly included in incident response and recovery planning.

changes.title: PR.AC-6: Identities proofed and bound to credentials
changes.area: PR — Protect / PR.AC — Identity Management and Access Control
changes.newText: Identities are proofed and bound to credentials and asserted in interactions.
changes.rationale: As identity-based attacks increased, NIST recognized the need to explicitly address identity proofing — verifying that a credential holder is who they claim to be before binding credentials. This supports zero trust principles.

changes.title: PR.AC-7: Risk-based authentication for users, devices, and assets
changes.area: PR — Protect / PR.AC — Identity Management and Access Control
changes.newText: Users, devices, and other assets are authenticated (e.g., single-factor, multi-factor) commensurate with the risk of the transaction (e.g., individuals' security and privacy risks and other organizational risks).
changes.rationale: Authentication strength should be calibrated to the risk of the transaction being authenticated. This subcategory introduces the concept of risk-based authentication, foreshadowing the strong MFA requirements that appear in subsequent framework and regulatory updates.

changes.title: Scope expanded beyond critical infrastructure
changes.oldText: This voluntary Framework for Reducing Cyber Risks to Critical Infrastructure provides a prioritized, flexible, repeatable, and cost-effective approach to manage cybersecurity-related risk for those processes, information, and systems directly involved in the delivery of Critical Infrastructure Services.
changes.newText: This publication describes a Cybersecurity Framework that provides guidance for industry, government agencies, and other organizations to better manage and reduce cybersecurity risk.
changes.rationale: The original framework was explicitly scoped to critical infrastructure organizations. Version 1.1 opened the door to use by any organization, acknowledging that the framework's principles are universally applicable. This was a significant conceptual shift completed in v2.0.

changes.title: ID.AM-6: Cybersecurity roles for all workforce clarified
changes.oldText: Cybersecurity roles and responsibilities for the entire workforce and third-party stakeholders (e.g., suppliers, customers, partners) are established.
changes.newText: Cybersecurity roles and responsibilities for the entire workforce and third-party stakeholders (e.g., suppliers, customers, partners) are established.
changes.rationale: Minor clarification to explicitly encompass third-party stakeholders in the definition of workforce roles. The accompanying guidance notes were expanded significantly.

changes.title: ID.RA-5: Threat, vulnerability, and impact language refined
changes.oldText: Threats, vulnerabilities, likelihoods, and impacts are used to determine risk.
changes.newText: Threats, vulnerabilities, likelihoods, and impacts are used to understand inherent risk and inform prioritization decisions.
changes.rationale: Added 'inherent risk' and 'prioritization decisions' to clarify the purpose and output of risk assessment — it drives prioritization, not just documentation.

changes.title: Informative References updated to current standards
changes.rationale: All informative references were reviewed and updated. NIST SP 800-53 references updated from Rev 3 to Rev 4. COBIT 4.1 references updated to COBIT 5. CIS Critical Security Controls updated to CSC v6.0. ISA 62443 updated to current edition. These updates ensure alignment with the most current supporting standards.

changes.title: Self-assessment guidance added
changes.rationale: Section 4.0 was expanded to include explicit guidance on how organizations can use the framework for self-assessment of their cybersecurity posture. This was a highly requested addition from the v1.0 stakeholder community.

changes.title: DE.CM-7: Monitoring for unauthorized personnel, connections, devices, and software clarified
changes.area: DE — Detect / DE.CM — Security Continuous Monitoring
changes.oldText: Monitoring for unauthorized personnel, connections, devices, and software is performed.
changes.newText: Monitoring for unauthorized personnel, connections, devices, and software is performed.
changes.rationale: Guidance notes clarified that this applies to both internal networks and cloud environments, and updated examples to include SaaS, IaaS, and mobile devices.

changes.title: PR.AT-1: Awareness training updated to include role-based content
changes.area: PR — Protect / PR.AT — Awareness and Training
changes.oldText: All users are informed and trained.
changes.newText: All users are informed and trained.
changes.rationale: Guidance notes expanded to explicitly address role-based training requirements, privilege users, and periodic awareness reminders. The base subcategory text was unchanged but implementation guidance was significantly enhanced.

changes.title: Privacy and civil liberties language added throughout
changes.rationale: Version 1.1 added explicit references to privacy and civil liberties considerations alongside cybersecurity risk management. This reflected growing regulatory attention to privacy (predating GDPR enforcement and CCPA by design) and set the stage for the integrated privacy controls in NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5.

changes.title: Framework Tiers clarified with supply chain integration
changes.rationale: The four Framework Tiers (Partial, Risk Informed, Repeatable, Adaptive) were updated to include supply chain risk management as a dimension of maturity across all tiers. Organizations at Tier 3+ are expected to have active supply chain risk management processes.

changes.title: ID.GV-4: Governance and risk management processes address cybersecurity risks
changes.oldText: Governance and risk management processes address cybersecurity risks.
changes.newText: Governance and risk management processes address cybersecurity risks.
changes.rationale: Guidance notes clarified the relationship between the organization's enterprise risk management (ERM) framework and cybersecurity risk management, setting the stage for the fuller integration in CSF 2.0's new Govern function.

changes.title: Metrics and measurement guidance expanded
changes.rationale: Appendix C (Glossary) and the implementation guidance were updated to provide clearer direction on how to define, collect, and use cybersecurity metrics within the framework. This addressed a common gap identified in v1.0 adoption: organizations knew what to do but not how to measure it.

changes.title: ID.RM-3: Risk tolerance informed by role in critical infrastructure clarified
changes.area: ID — Identify / ID.RM — Risk Management Strategy
changes.oldText: The organization's determination of risk tolerance is informed by its role in critical infrastructure and sector specific risk analysis.
changes.newText: The organization's determination of risk tolerance is informed by its role in critical infrastructure and sector specific risk analysis.
changes.rationale: Guidance notes added to clarify how organizations operating across multiple sectors should determine risk tolerance when sector-specific guidance conflicts or overlaps.

highlights: Brand new GOVERN (GV) Function added as the 6th Function — covers organizational context, risk management strategy, roles, policy, oversight, and supply chain governance
Scope expanded from 'critical infrastructure' to ALL organizations of any size, sector, or maturity level — the word 'critical infrastructure' removed from the framework title
Categories reorganized: ID reduced from 6 to 3 categories as ID.BE, ID.GV, ID.RM, and ID.SC content moved into the new GV function
PR.AC renamed to PR.AA (Identity Management, Authentication and Access Control) with updated content. New PR.PS (Platform Security) and PR.IR (Technology Infrastructure Resilience) categories added
Implementation Examples added to every subcategory — providing practical, non-prescriptive guidance on how to implement each requirement
RS.RP (Response Planning) renamed to RS.MA (Incident Management). DE.DP (Detection Processes) merged into DE.CM. RC.IM and RS.IM content moved into GV.OV

changes.title: New GOVERN (GV) Function — 6th Function of the CSF
changes.newText: The GOVERN Function addresses an organization's cybersecurity risk management strategy, expectations, and policy. The GOVERN Function provides outcomes to inform what an organization may do to achieve and prioritize the outcomes of the other five Functions in the context of its mission and stakeholder expectations.
changes.rationale: NIST found that the original 5 functions lacked a home for governance-level activities — strategy, policy, and organizational context. In practice, organizations were struggling to embed cybersecurity into enterprise risk management. The GV function directly addresses this by making governance an equal peer to the technical functions.

changes.title: GV.OC: Organizational Context — new category
changes.newText: The circumstances surrounding the organization's cybersecurity risk management decisions are understood (organizational mission; stakeholder expectations; dependencies and critical services; legal, regulatory, and contractual requirements).
changes.rationale: Effective cybersecurity risk management requires understanding the organization's mission, its dependencies, and the context in which it operates. GV.OC consolidates content previously scattered across ID.BE and adds new stakeholder-expectation requirements.

changes.title: GV.OC-01: Organizational mission informs cybersecurity risk management
changes.newText: The organizational mission is understood and informs cybersecurity risk management.
changes.rationale: Cybersecurity investments and priorities must be anchored to the organization's mission. Without this alignment, security becomes an end in itself rather than a means to protect what matters.

changes.title: GV.OC-03: Legal, regulatory, and contractual cybersecurity requirements understood
changes.newText: Legal, regulatory, and contractual requirements regarding cybersecurity — including privacy and civil liberties obligations — are understood and managed.
changes.rationale: Organizations operate in increasingly complex regulatory environments (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI DSS, CMMC, state privacy laws). CSF 2.0 makes it explicit that understanding and managing these obligations is a core governance responsibility.

changes.title: GV.RM: Risk Management Strategy — new category (moved from ID.RM)
changes.newText: The organization's priorities, constraints, risk tolerance and appetite statements, and assumptions are established, communicated, and used to support operational risk decisions.
changes.rationale: Risk management strategy is fundamentally a governance function. Moving it from ID (Identify) to GV (Govern) clarifies that establishing risk appetite and tolerance is an executive/board-level responsibility, not a technical one.

changes.title: GV.RM-02: Risk appetite and tolerance statements established and communicated
changes.area: GV — Govern / GV.RM — Risk Management Strategy
changes.newText: Risk appetite and risk tolerance statements are established, communicated, and maintained.
changes.rationale: Many organizations manage risk intuitively without formally stating what level of risk is acceptable. This subcategory requires explicit, documented, communicated risk appetite statements — enabling consistent risk-based decision-making at all levels.

changes.title: GV.RM-07: Strategic opportunities (positive risks) included in risk discussions
changes.area: GV — Govern / GV.RM — Risk Management Strategy
changes.newText: Strategic opportunities (i.e., positive risks) are characterized and are included in organizational cybersecurity risk discussions.
changes.rationale: This is a notable addition — it acknowledges that risk management is not purely defensive. Organizations should also consider upside risks (e.g., adopting new secure technologies, cloud transformation opportunities) as part of their cybersecurity risk strategy.

changes.title: GV.RR: Roles, Responsibilities, and Authorities — new category
changes.newText: Cybersecurity roles, responsibilities, and authorities to foster accountability, performance assessment, and continuous improvement are established and communicated.
changes.rationale: Accountability for cybersecurity must be clearly defined and enforced. GV.RR consolidates and expands on content from the former ID.GV category, making explicit that leadership is accountable and that adequate resources must be allocated.

changes.title: GV.RR-01: Leadership accountable for cybersecurity risk; culture is risk-aware
changes.area: GV — Govern / GV.RR — Roles, Responsibilities, and Authorities
changes.newText: Organizational leadership is responsible and accountable for cybersecurity risk and fosters a culture that is risk-aware, ethical, and continually improving.
changes.rationale: Board and C-suite accountability for cybersecurity risk is now explicitly required. This reflects the regulatory environment in 2024 — the SEC's cybersecurity disclosure rules and FTC enforcement actions have made board-level accountability a legal expectation, not just a best practice.

changes.title: GV.RR-03: Adequate resources allocated for cybersecurity
changes.area: GV — Govern / GV.RR — Roles, Responsibilities, and Authorities
changes.newText: Adequate resources are allocated commensurate with the cybersecurity risk strategy, roles, responsibilities, and policies.
changes.rationale: A common failure mode is assigning cybersecurity responsibilities without providing the budget, staff, or tools to execute them. This subcategory makes resource allocation an explicit governance obligation.

changes.title: GV.PO: Policy — new category
changes.newText: Organizational cybersecurity policy is established, communicated, and enforced.
changes.rationale: Policy is the mechanism by which governance translates into operational expectations. GV.PO elevates policy management to a formal governance category with requirements for both establishing policy (GV.PO-01) and keeping it current (GV.PO-02).

changes.title: GV.OV: Oversight — new category (absorbs RS.IM and RC.IM content)
changes.newText: Results of organization-wide cybersecurity risk management activities and performance are used to inform, improve, and adjust the risk management strategy.
changes.rationale: The oversight loop — measuring outcomes and feeding them back into strategy — was missing as an explicit category. GV.OV also absorbs the improvement content from RS.IM and RC.IM, centralizing the continuous-improvement function under governance rather than spreading it across response and recovery.

changes.title: GV.SC: Cybersecurity Supply Chain Risk Management — elevated to Govern
changes.newText: Cyber supply chain risk management processes are identified, prioritized, established, assessed, managed, monitored, and improved by organizational stakeholders.
changes.rationale: Supply chain risk management moved from an operational concern (ID.SC) to a governance function (GV.SC). This reflects the evolution of supply chain attacks (SolarWinds, Log4j) from theoretical risk to a primary attack vector requiring board-level attention. GV.SC has 10 subcategories — the most of any GV category.

changes.title: GV.SC-06: Due diligence before entering supplier relationships
changes.area: GV — Govern / GV.SC — Cybersecurity Supply Chain Risk Management
changes.newText: Planning and due diligence are performed to reduce risks before entering into formal supplier or other third-party relationships.
changes.rationale: New in CSF 2.0 — the original ID.SC focused on managing existing supplier relationships. GV.SC-06 adds a pre-relationship due diligence requirement, addressing risk before contracts are signed.

changes.title: ID.IM: Improvement — new category in Identify function
changes.newText: Improvements to organizational cybersecurity risk management processes, procedures and activities are identified across all CSF Functions.
changes.rationale: CSF 2.0 added a dedicated Improvement category to the Identify function. This consolidates the requirement to continuously assess and improve cybersecurity practices using lessons learned, not just from incidents (as in the former RS.IM/RC.IM) but from all sources including exercises, assessments, and audits.

changes.title: PR.PS: Platform Security — new category (replaces PR.IP and PR.PT)
changes.newText: The hardware, software (e.g., firmware, operating systems, applications), and services of physical and virtual platforms are managed consistent with the organization's risk strategy to protect their confidentiality, integrity, and availability.
changes.rationale: PR.IP (Information Protection Processes) and PR.PT (Protective Technology) were merged into PR.PS (Platform Security), recognizing that security controls for hardware, software, and services are unified in modern environments. The new name better reflects the scope: protecting the platform, not just 'information.'

changes.title: PR.IR: Technology Infrastructure Resilience — new category
changes.newText: Security architectures are managed with the organization's risk strategy to protect asset confidentiality, integrity, and availability, and organizational resilience.
changes.rationale: Carved out from the former PR.IP, PR.IR focuses specifically on resilience — the ability to withstand and recover from disruptions. This reflects the growing importance of resilience architecture (redundancy, failover, secure-by-design) as a protective measure, distinct from hardening and configuration management.

changes.title: PR.AC renamed to PR.AA — Identity Management, Authentication and Access Control
changes.oldText: Access to physical and logical assets and associated facilities is limited to authorized users, processes, and devices, and is managed consistent with the assessed risk of unauthorized access to authorized activities and transactions.
changes.newText: Access to physical and logical assets is limited to authorized users, services, and hardware and managed commensurate with the assessed risk of unauthorized access.
changes.rationale: Renamed from PR.AC (Access Control) to PR.AA (Identity Management, Authentication and Access Control) to better reflect the full scope: the category covers identity lifecycle management and authentication — not just access control policy. The rename aligns with modern zero-trust language.

changes.title: RS.RP renamed to RS.MA — Incident Management
changes.oldText: Response processes and procedures are executed and maintained, to ensure response to detected cybersecurity incidents.
changes.newText: Responses to detected cybersecurity incidents are managed.
changes.rationale: RS.RP (Response Planning) was renamed to RS.MA (Incident Management) to better reflect that this category covers active management of incidents — not just planning. The original name implied the response plan was the output; the new name makes clear that active incident management is the goal.

changes.title: ID.BE (Business Environment) → GV.OC (Organizational Context)
changes.oldText: The organization's mission, objectives, stakeholders, and activities are understood and prioritized; this information is used to inform cybersecurity roles, responsibilities, and risk management decisions.
changes.rationale: Understanding the organizational mission and stakeholder landscape is a governance activity, not just an identification activity. The content was restructured into GV.OC with expanded requirements for legal/regulatory context and stakeholder expectations.

changes.title: ID.GV (Governance) → split across GV.PO, GV.RR, and GV.OV
changes.oldText: The policies, procedures, and processes to manage and monitor the organization's regulatory, legal, risk, environmental, and operational requirements are understood and inform the management of cybersecurity risk.
changes.rationale: The former ID.GV category was too broad. CSF 2.0 splits it into dedicated categories: GV.PO for policy, GV.RR for roles and accountability, and GV.OV for oversight and improvement — each with its own requirements and subcategories.

changes.title: ID.RM (Risk Management Strategy) → GV.RM
changes.oldText: The organization's priorities, constraints, risk tolerances, and assumptions are established and used to support operational risk decisions.
changes.rationale: Risk management strategy is a governance function, not purely an identification activity. Moved to GV.RM with expanded requirements including explicit risk appetite statements (GV.RM-02) and enterprise risk integration (GV.RM-03).

changes.title: ID.SC (Supply Chain Risk Management) → GV.SC
changes.oldText: The organization's priorities, constraints, risk tolerances, and assumptions are established and used to support risk decisions associated with managing supply chain risk.
changes.rationale: Supply chain risk management was elevated from an operational category in ID (Identify) to a full governance category in GV (Govern). This reflects the strategic importance of supply chain security post-SolarWinds. GV.SC has 10 subcategories vs. ID.SC's 5, with new requirements for pre-relationship due diligence and lifecycle management.

changes.title: PR.IP (Information Protection) → PR.PS and PR.IR
changes.oldText: Information and records (data) are managed consistent with the organization's risk strategy to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information.
changes.rationale: PR.IP was a catch-all category that contained both platform hardening activities (now PR.PS) and resilience architecture activities (now PR.IR). Splitting these reflects how modern security teams organize their work: platform security teams handle hardening, while resilience/business continuity teams handle availability architecture.

changes.title: PR.MA (Maintenance) → PR.PS (Platform Security)
changes.oldText: Maintenance and repairs of organizational assets is performed consistent with policies and procedures.
changes.rationale: Maintenance activities (patching, updates, authorized maintenance access) are part of platform security management. Merging PR.MA into PR.PS eliminates the separate category while preserving the requirements within a more logically coherent grouping.

changes.title: PR.PT (Protective Technology) → PR.PS (Platform Security)
changes.oldText: Technical security solutions are managed to ensure the security and resilience of systems and assets, consistent with related policies, procedures, and agreements.
changes.rationale: Protective technology (firewalls, logging, network segmentation) is an integral part of platform security. Merging PR.PT into PR.PS creates a unified platform security category covering hardening, configuration, maintenance, and technical controls.

changes.title: DE.DP (Detection Processes) → DE.CM (Continuous Monitoring)
changes.oldText: Detection processes and procedures are maintained and tested to ensure awareness of anomalous events.
changes.rationale: Detection processes and continuous monitoring are inseparable in practice. DE.DP requirements (testing detection processes, establishing roles for detection, communicating event information) were merged into DE.CM, simplifying the Detect function from 3 categories to 2.

changes.title: RS.IM (Improvements) → GV.OV (Oversight)
changes.oldText: Organizational response activities are improved by incorporating lessons learned from current and previous detection/response activities.
changes.rationale: Lessons learned and improvement activities are governance-level oversight responsibilities — they should drive strategy updates, not just operational process changes. Moving RS.IM to GV.OV reinforces that the oversight loop is a leadership function.

changes.title: RC.IM (Improvements) → GV.OV (Oversight)
changes.oldText: Recovery planning and processes are improved by incorporating lessons learned into future activities.
changes.rationale: Same as RS.IM — improvement activities are now consolidated under GV.OV. This eliminates redundancy between RS.IM and RC.IM, which had overlapping improvement requirements, and places the improvement function squarely under governance oversight.

changes.title: Scope expanded: 'critical infrastructure' removed from title and scope
changes.oldText: Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity
changes.newText: Cybersecurity Framework — applicable to all organizations of any size, in any sector, at any cybersecurity maturity level
changes.rationale: CSF 2.0 completes the scope expansion begun in 1.1. The framework is now explicitly for all organizations — not just those in the original 16 critical infrastructure sectors. The title change from 'Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity' to 'Cybersecurity Framework' signals this universal applicability.

changes.title: Implementation Examples added to every subcategory
changes.rationale: CSF 2.0 adds Implementation Examples to every one of the 106 subcategories. These are non-prescriptive, real-world examples of how organizations might achieve each subcategory outcome. This was one of the most-requested additions from the v1.1 stakeholder community — practitioners wanted practical 'what does this look like?' guidance.

changes.title: PR.AC-1 → PR.AA-01: Identity and credential management updated
changes.area: PR — Protect / PR.AA — Identity Management, Authentication and Access Control
changes.oldText: Identities and credentials are issued, managed, verified, revoked, and audited for authorized devices, users and processes.
changes.newText: Identities and credentials for authorized users, services, and hardware are managed by the organization.
changes.rationale: The updated text explicitly includes 'services' (service accounts, API keys, machine identities) alongside users and hardware — reflecting the reality that machine-to-machine and service-to-service authentication is now a major attack surface.

changes.title: ID.AM-01: Asset inventory expanded to include data and software
changes.oldText: Physical devices and systems within the organization are inventoried.
changes.newText: Inventories of hardware managed by the organization are maintained.
changes.rationale: CSF 2.0 splits the old ID.AM-1 and ID.AM-2 requirements and adds new subcategories for software, data, and services. The expanded asset management category reflects that modern asset inventories must cover hardware, software, data, services, and personnel.

changes.title: ID.AM-07: Data inventories maintained
changes.newText: Inventories of data and corresponding metadata for designated data types are maintained.
changes.rationale: Data is an asset class that must be inventoried just like hardware and software. This new subcategory explicitly requires maintaining data inventories with metadata — essential for data governance, privacy compliance, and knowing what to protect.

changes.title: ID.AM-08: Systems/products/services and their components inventoried
changes.newText: Systems, hardware, software, services, and data are managed throughout their life cycles.
changes.rationale: Lifecycle management of all asset types is now an explicit requirement. This encompasses procurement, deployment, operation, and decommissioning — addressing the common security gap of systems that outlive their intended lifecycle.

changes.title: ID.RA-01: Vulnerabilities identified including software and services
changes.oldText: Asset vulnerabilities are identified and documented.
changes.newText: Vulnerabilities in assets are identified, validated, and recorded.
changes.rationale: The addition of 'validated' is significant — it requires confirmation that a reported vulnerability actually affects the organization's specific configuration before logging it. This reduces false-positive noise in vulnerability management programs.

changes.title: DE.AE-02: Anomalous activity analyzed for attack patterns
changes.area: DE — Detect / DE.AE — Adverse Event Analysis
changes.oldText: Detected events are analyzed to understand attack targets and methods.
changes.newText: Potentially adverse events are analyzed to better understand associated activities.
changes.rationale: The updated text broadens scope from 'detected events' to 'potentially adverse events' — acknowledging that detection happens at different confidence levels and that analysis should begin before confirmation of malicious activity.

changes.title: DE.AE-06: Information on adverse events shared with authorized parties
changes.area: DE — Detect / DE.AE — Adverse Event Analysis
changes.newText: Information on adverse events is provided to authorized staff and tools.
changes.rationale: Detection is not complete until information reaches those who can act on it. This new subcategory makes information sharing within the organization an explicit detection requirement, not just a response assumption.

changes.title: RS.CO-05: Voluntary sharing of incident information with external stakeholders
changes.area: RS — Respond / RS.CO — Incident Response Reporting and Communication
changes.oldText: Voluntary information sharing occurs with external stakeholders to achieve broader cybersecurity situational awareness.
changes.newText: Sharing of cyber threat intelligence and other cybersecurity information with designated parties is coordinated consistent with established policies.
changes.rationale: Updated to reflect the maturation of threat intelligence sharing programs (ISACs, CISA partnerships). The new language clarifies that sharing should be coordinated and policy-driven, not ad hoc.

changes.title: RC.CO-04: Recovery activities communicated to internal and external stakeholders
changes.area: RC — Recover / RC.CO — Incident Recovery Communication
changes.oldText: Recovery activities are communicated to internal and external stakeholders as well as executive and management teams.
changes.newText: Recovery activities and the status of restoration of capabilities and services are communicated to designated parties.
changes.rationale: Language updated to be less prescriptive about who receives updates (not just executives) and more focused on what is communicated: the status of capability restoration. This reflects the reality that stakeholder communication during recovery varies by organization type and incident severity.

changes.title: GV.SC-08: Suppliers included in incident response and recovery (expanded)
changes.area: GV — Govern / GV.SC — Cybersecurity Supply Chain Risk Management
changes.oldText: Response and recovery planning and testing are conducted with suppliers and third-party providers.
changes.newText: Relevant suppliers and other third parties are included in incident planning, response, and recovery activities.
changes.rationale: The v1.1 requirement was limited to 'planning and testing.' GV.SC-08 expands this to include actual incident response and recovery activities, recognizing that supply chain incidents require real-time coordination with suppliers, not just pre-planned exercises.

changes.title: Profiles concept updated with Community Profiles guidance
changes.rationale: CSF 2.0 introduces the concept of 'Community Profiles' — shared baseline profiles developed by industry sectors, regulators, or consortia that organizations can adopt or adapt. NIST has published several reference profiles alongside CSF 2.0 (e.g., for small businesses, enterprise IT, critical infrastructure sectors). This makes the Profile mechanism more practical for organizations that lack resources to build one from scratch.

changes.title: Framework Tiers refined with supply chain and governance dimensions
changes.rationale: The four Framework Tiers (Partial, Risk-Informed, Repeatable, Adaptive) were updated in CSF 2.0 to explicitly include dimensions for supply chain risk management and governance maturity at each tier level. An organization at Tier 3 (Repeatable) must now demonstrate repeatable governance processes, not just repeatable technical controls.

changes.title: Informative References replaced with online NIST Reference Tool
changes.rationale: CSF 1.1 included a static table of Informative References in the appendix. CSF 2.0 removes this static appendix and replaces it with a dynamic online reference tool (the NIST CSF 2.0 Reference Tool at csrc.nist.gov). This allows NIST to update mappings to other standards (SP 800-53, ISO 27001, CIS Controls) without publishing a new framework version. A significant improvement for keeping cross-standard mappings current.

changes.title: PR.AA-05: Access permissions reviewed and removed upon role changes
changes.area: PR — Protect / PR.AA — Identity Management, Authentication and Access Control
changes.newText: Access permissions, entitlements, and authorizations are defined in a policy, managed, enforced, and reviewed, and incorporate the principles of least privilege and separation of duties.
changes.rationale: CSF 2.0 makes entitlement management and periodic access reviews an explicit requirement. Stale access permissions are one of the most common causes of insider threat and privilege escalation. The new text requires both definition and periodic review of access rights.

changes.title: PR.PS-04: Logs generated and made available to support analysis
changes.newText: Audit/log records are created, protected, configured, and retained to enable the detection, investigation, and recovery from adverse events.
changes.rationale: Logging requirements are now explicitly part of the Protect function — not just implied by the Detect function. This reflects the reality that logs must be generated and protected before they can be used for detection and investigation.

changes.title: PR.PS-06: Secure software development practices used
changes.newText: Secure software development practices are integrated into the software development life cycle.
changes.rationale: Secure-by-design software development is now an explicit CSF subcategory. This aligns with the Biden and Biden-era executive orders on software security and reflects the growing 'shift-left' security movement requiring security to be embedded in development, not bolted on afterward.

changes.title: DE.CM-09: Computing hardware and software monitored for unauthorized changes
changes.newText: Computing hardware and software, runtime environments, and their data are monitored to find potentially adverse events.
changes.rationale: File integrity monitoring and runtime environment monitoring are now explicit detection requirements. The inclusion of 'runtime environments' addresses container, serverless, and cloud workload monitoring — attack surfaces that didn't exist when CSF 1.0 was written.
